title: A Non-Isotropic Mechanism for the Formation of Trapped Surfaces
speaker: Sergiu Klainerman
abstract: I present a new, fully anisotropic, criterion for formation of trapped surfaces in vacuum obtained in collaboration with J. Luk and I. Rodnianski. We provide conditions on null data, concentrated in a neighborhood of a short null geodesic segment (possibly flat everywhere else) whose future development contains a trapped surface. This extends considerably the previous result of Christodoulou which required instead a uniform condition along all null geodesic generators.

proof_stages.label: Trapped surfaces and Penrose mechanism
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls Penrose's theorem, defines closed trapped surfaces by negativity of both null expansions, and explains via Raychaudhuri's equation why such a surface forces future null geodesic incompleteness.

proof_stages.label: Characteristic double-null short-pulse setup
proof_stages.summary: Characteristic data are prescribed on two intersecting null hypersurfaces, extended to a double-null foliation with shears as key data, and normalized into short-pulse classes allowing large but controlled initial shear.

proof_stages.label: Christodoulou short-pulse black box and trace formula
proof_stages.summary: The existing semiglobal existence estimates for short-pulse data are used as a black box, and integrating the Raychaudhuri and shear transport equations gives an approximate formula for the outgoing expansion in terms of the integrated shear, yielding Christodoulou's uniform trapped-surface criterion.

proof_stages.label: Non-isotropic trapping theorem
proof_stages.summary: The new result replaces Christodoulou's uniform lower bound on the integrated shear by a localized/supremum condition and seeks a trapped surface not among the double-null leaves, motivated by the earlier localized 'scar' phenomenon.

proof_stages.label: Deformation to an elliptic trappedness condition
proof_stages.summary: On the final incoming null hypersurface the desired surface is written as a graph r(omega), and the expansion of this deformed surface is computed so that negativity reduces to a scalar elliptic inequality involving r and the integrated shear m0.

proof_stages.label: Solving the elliptic inequality from localized shear
proof_stages.summary: Writing r=e^{-phi}, the speaker constructs a suitable phi using the Green function of an elliptic operator on the sphere, a cutoff near the concentrated direction, and an added constant to satisfy the inequality both outside and inside the small cap.

proof_stages.label: Geometric mechanism and conclusion
proof_stages.summary: The proof is synthesized as a two-part mechanism: a deformation makes the expansion negative almost everywhere, and the highly concentrated initial disturbance supplies exactly the missing negativity near the remaining direction, producing a trapped surface.

examples.name: Flat Minkowski comparison and almost-trapped deformation
examples.note: Flat spacetime first illustrates why ordinary surfaces are not trapped, and later the observation that one can deform a Minkowski surface to be trapped except near a point is presented as the intuition for adding a localized shear push.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker computes with the Minkowski/deformed-surface setting, deriving the trace-expansion inequality, integrating it to rule out trapped surfaces in flat Minkowski space, and constructing a deformation that is trapped except near a point.

passes.reason: Minkowski is the flat maximally symmetric trivial vacuum model, used to show trapped-surface formation/inequality cannot occur without the pulse.
Minkowski space is the flat trivial maximally symmetric vacuum model, used here as the obstruction showing the trapped-surface inequality cannot hold without the nonzero pulse.
Flat Minkowski spacetime is the maximally symmetric trivial vacuum solution and is used as the flat no-trapped-surface obstruction/comparison, with only an almost-trapped deformation possible.

Minkowski space is the flat maximally symmetric/trivial vacuum model, used as the zero-disturbance obstruction where the trapped-surface inequality cannot hold, with the deformation only reaching an almost-trapped boundary case.
Minkowski space is the flat maximally symmetric trivial vacuum model, used as the no-trapped-surface obstruction while the deformation is only a limiting almost-trapped construction.
Minkowski is the flat/trivial maximally symmetric zero-shear model, with the almost-trapped deformation a limiting case, and it is used as the obstruction showing full trappedness cannot occur there.

Flat Minkowski space is the trivial zero-curvature, maximally symmetric vacuum baseline, used to show the trapped-surface inequality cannot be globally satisfied there except in an almost-trapped deformation.

Flat Minkowski is the trivial maximally symmetric vacuum background, used as the zero-shear obstruction where full trapped surfaces cannot occur, while the deformation is only an almost-trapped limiting case.
Minkowski is the maximally symmetric flat/trivial vacuum case, and the limiting almost-trapped deformation is used to show genuine trapped surfaces fail without nonzero focusing.
Minkowski is the flat maximally symmetric trivial vacuum spacetime, used to show the trapped-surface inequality cannot hold there except in an almost-trapped deformation.
Flat Minkowski is the maximally symmetric trivial vacuum model, and the deformation is a limiting almost-trapped obstruction used to show full trapping fails in flat space.
Minkowski space is the trivial flat, maximally symmetric vacuum solution used to show the trapped-surface inequality cannot hold globally there

examples.name: Uniformization-equation analogy
examples.note: An audience comparison with uniformization helps make the final elliptic inequality familiar, but the speaker only notes the analogy and says the signs make the present problem simpler.
examples.engagement_reason: The uniformization equation is only invoked as an analogy to the elliptic inequality, with comments about signs, but no uniformization calculation or construction is carried out.

passes.reason: The uniformization equation is a standard elliptic-PDE analogy for the deformation inequality, not used as a disproof.
